title: UK Government Gives More Money to BBC World Service
summary: The UK government is giving more money to the BBC World Service, which is a news service that shares stories with people all over the world. They will add £33 million over three years, which is about 8% more than before. The foreign secretary, Yvette Cooper, says this helps because the World Service gives clear and true news where it is hard to find. The BBC is happy because this money lets them keep telling trusted stories and fight fake news. The World Service reaches over 300 million people each week, and many live in places where news is not free. The government pays about one-third of the cost, and the rest comes from TV licence fees. The BBC wants the government to pay for all of it again, like they did until 2014. This funding is important to help people get good news, especially in countries like Iran where the BBC is banned but many still listen.

questions.question: In which country is the BBC banned but many people still listen?

background_read: The BBC World Service is a part of the BBC that shares news in many languages with people all over the world. It helps give true information, especially in places where it is hard to find good news. The UK government gives money to support this service because they think it is important for fighting fake news and helping people learn what is happening. This article talks about how the government is increasing this money to make sure the World Service can keep working well.
Article_Structure: WHO: The UK government and the BBC World Service
WHAT: The government is increasing funding by £33 million over three years
WHERE: This affects global audiences, especially in countries with little media freedom like Iran
WHY: To support trusted journalism and counter disinformation worldwide

perspectives.description: The government believes funding the BBC World Service is crucial for providing reliable news and fighting disinformation globally.
The BBC welcomes the funding but wants full government support to ensure long-term stability and independence.
